channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: S.E.C. Proposes to Kill Climate Change Disclosure Rule
item.description: The regulation would have required all publicly traded companies to disclose whether they faced significant risks from climate change and its effects.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil drilling site in Wyoming. The S.E.C. rule would have required companies to disclose their contributions to climate change and the risks they faced from a warming planet.

item.title: Can New Technology Help Save North Atlantic Right Whales?
item.description: Innovative systems to keep ships from hitting North Atlantic right whales are coming into use. The Trump administration is weighing whether they can replace a bedrock protection.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Diesel Fuel Spilled on National Mall During 250th Event Setup
item.description: The Trump administration blamed the two spills on vandalism, though the U.S. Park Police is still investigating the incidents.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:description: Construction on the Great American State Fair on the National Mall earlier this month. The spills were detected during setup operations for the fair.

item.title: Greenpeace’s Long War With a Pipeline Titan Enters a Strange New Phase
item.description: This month, a North Dakota court barred Greenpeace from saying what it wanted in a European court, an unusual move. The environmental group says it is forging ahead.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: A court building in Amsterdam. The North Dakota Supreme Court this month barred Greenpeace International from making claims against Energy Transfer in a Dutch countersuit.

item.title: Drilling Into the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica
item.description: Ten people. Eight weeks. Three thousand feet to pierce a fast-melting Antarctic glacier.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Chang W. Lee
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Drilling equipment on the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica in January.

item.title: As Climate Change Extends Europe’s Heat Season, Schools Bake
item.description: Classrooms can be particularly vulnerable amid climate change, as the hottest times of the year increasingly overlap with the academic calendar.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan and Léontine Gallois
item.media:description: Classes ended early at a school in Soustons, France, on Thursday amid the European heat wave.

item.title: Trump Lifts Restrictions on Off-Road Vehicles on Public Lands
item.description: The president rescinded two executive orders that led to prohibitions on the use of the vehicles in most national parks.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: A horse crosses a road in Big Bend National Park in Texas in March.

item.title: A Clean Energy PAC Helped Beat Chip Roy, and Now It Has New Targets
item.description: A PAC representing wind and solar energy interests spent $1.1 million to boost the Republican primary opponent of Chip Roy, an opponent of renewables. Now they are trying to save a Republican ally in Iowa.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of renewable energy investors paid for this projection on the side of the Energy Department building in Washington gloating over Representative Chip Roy’s defeat in a Republican primary for Texas attorney general.

item.title: Europe’s Heat Wave Has the ‘Fingerprints of Climate Change All Over It’
item.description: An unusually early heat wave in Europe, new technology to save whales and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: The Tuileries Garden in Paris on Tuesday during an early heat wave.

item.title: The long voyage helped a Times journalist better understand his late father.
item.description: A chance discovery aboard the research vessel led to a kind of reconnection.
item.dc:creator: Chang W. Lee
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: The author, Chang W. Lee, left; his brother, Chang H. Lee; and mother, Jun-hui Kwak, in a photo Chang’s father kept with him on his long voyages.

item.title: National Park Entrance Fees Are Funding Trump’s D.C. Projects
item.description: The administration is spending at least $67 million worth of fees paid by visitors to national parks on fixing D.C. fountains and the Reflecting Pool.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Andrea Fuller
item.media:credit: Brontë Wittpenn/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The National Park Service is spending millions in fees paid by visitors to parks across the country, like Yosemite National Park in California, to pay for restoration of nine ornamental fountains in Washington, D.C.

item.title: Heat Wave Hits Canada With Warnings in Alberta, Manitoba and Saskatchewan
item.description: Canada’s first big heat wave of the year was expected to bring prolonged warm weather to southern Alberta, Saskatchewan and Manitoba.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff

item.title: Hunting a ‘Frankenfish’ With a Bow and Arrow
item.description: Invasive snakeheads are proliferating in waterways from New York to Florida. Wildlife officials around the Chesapeake Bay are recommending a solution: high-powered compound bows.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler and Caroline Gutman
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times

item.title: When Will Emissions From Air Travel Start to Fall?
item.description: How the war in Iran is affecting greenhouse gas emissions from air travel, a look back at “An Inconvenient Truth” and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: One research firm estimates that if the Strait of Hormuz doesn’t reopen soon, total jet fuel demand could actually fall this year as airlines cut flights.

item.title: U.S. Aims to Give Cold War Plutonium to Start-Ups for Nuclear Fuel
item.description: Companies say it’s a better way to dispose of the Cold War-era material — and fix a shortage of nuclear fuel. But the plan has also faced criticism from nonproliferation experts.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: Work with radioactive materials at a plutonium facility at the at Los Alamos National Laboratory in 1978.

item.title: Scientists Ditched a Scary Climate Scenario. What Now?
item.description: While global warming is still a threat, the decision to back away from a worst-case outlook raises questions about whether some risks have been overstated.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Eric Niiler
item.media:description: A wildfire burning in the Simi Valley of California this month.

item.title: New York Leads in Controlling Heat-Trapping Methane. That Could Change.
item.description: An overhaul of the state’s 2019 climate law proposed by Gov. Kathy Hochul would alter how the potent greenhouse gas is measured.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:description: Gov. Kathy Hochul has said that a potential shift in how New York measures methane would align it with most other states.

item.title: One of Madagascar’s Oldest Sacred Trees Is Slowly Dying
item.description: In a forest in Madagascar, the demise of a centuries-old baobab points to the fraying of a fragile ecosystem.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe

item.title: Twenty Years After His Film, Al Gore Tweaks the Climate Script
item.description: Mr. Gore is still giving the slide show that “An Inconvenient Truth” was built around, but with changes that reflect a shift in the discussion of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan

item.title: How Wine, Truffles and Honey Could Help Europe Fight Wildfires
item.description: Countries are learning that the finer things in life sometimes have a serendipitous side benefit.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: Rafe Pomerance, the Paul Revere of Climate Change, Dies at 79
item.description: An environmental lobbyist and activist, he was a pivotal figure in drawing public attention and political support to the existential issue.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Rafe Pomerance as president of the environmental group Friends of the Earth, in 1983. “He recruited us and energized us and gave us facts that allowed us to work on the most important issue of our time,” said Daniel Becker, a longtime leader with the Sierra Club.

item.title: Can Concrete Molds Revive Coral Reefs Ruined by Bombs and Climate Change?
item.description: In a devastated section of the Coral Triangle in the Pacific Ocean, a conservation group is trying to build an artificial reef.
item.dc:creator: Zunaira Saieed and Sirachai Arunrugstichai
item.media:description: Robin Philippo, the managing director of the Tropical Research and Conservation Center, installing an artificial reef structure near Pom Pom Island in Malaysia.

item.title: How ‘Sentinel Gardens’ Help Spot Dangerous Bugs Abroad
item.description: Scientists have planted American trees in China, Korea and elsewhere to attract hungry insects. Their hope is to identify the most damaging bugs before they cross the ocean.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: A citrus long-horned beetle on a sugar maple in a “sentinel garden” in China.

item.title: A Strong El Niño Could Be Coming. Countries Are Already Preparing.
item.description: Experts say a powerful El Niño weather pattern could be forming, which could put serious strain on a planet that is already dangerously heating up.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: A fisherman beside the Magdalena River in Colombia amid a severe drought during the 2016 El Niño.

item.title: A Very Lonely Caterpillar, Possibly the Last of Its Kind, Has Died
item.description: The Sacramento Mountains checkerspot butterfly hasn’t been seen in the wild since 2022. The caterpillar was the last individual in human care.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Diana Cervantes for The New York Times

item.title: When Humans Went Away, the Wildlife Strayed
item.description: When people disappeared from the landscape, as they did during the pandemic, wild animals changed how they used space and resources, scientists found.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mule deer captured by a trail camera as it walked along a game trail in the Verdugo Mountains in Glendale, Calif.

item.title: NOAA’s 2026 Atlantic Hurricane Forecast Sees Fewer Storms, but There’s a Catch
item.description: El Niño may keep the Atlantic quieter, but it could bring more storms to the Pacific.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:description: A satellite image showing Hurricane Melissa approaching Jamaica last October. Melissa was one of three Category 5 hurricanes last year.

item.title: Trump Eases Restrictions on Climate ‘Super Pollutants’
item.description: The administration is delaying a phaseout of hydrofluorocarbons, potent planet-warming chemicals used in air-conditioning and refrigeration.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: Replacing an air conditioning unit outside a home in western Pennsylvania during a heat wave last June.

item.title: A Powerful El Niño Is Forming. If History Is a Guide, It Could Hit Hard.
item.description: The biggest episodes of the past have altered the course of human events, according to researchers. An emerging one is drawing historic comparisons.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: An illustration published in 1877 depicted a scene in southern India. Millions died from a famine that coincided with an El Niño that year.

item.title: The decline of the Thwaites Glacier could affect tens of millions worldwide.
item.description: A collapse would sharply accelerate sea level rise in coastal cities.
item.dc:creator: Mira Rojanasakul

item.title: Europe’s Green-Energy Future Has a Reindeer Problem
item.description: In northern Norway, Sami people fear a copper mine will disrupt their traditional lifestyles.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil, Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Michal Siarek
item.media:credit: Michał Siarek for The New York Times

item.title: The New ‘Gold Rush’ of Geothermal Energy
item.description: A new wave of start-ups are trying to harvest emissions-free energy from inside the Earth, but the industry still faces significant challenges.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Executives of Fervo and others celebrated the geothermal company’s I.P.O. on the Nasdaq on May 13.

item.title: Fish and Wildlife Service Clears a Weedkiller, Saying It Won’t Cause Extinction
item.description: The finding effectively paves the way for continued use of atrazine, a widely used herbicide that has been linked to birth defects and cancer in humans.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:description: Test plots at a Syngenta research site in Junction City, Kan. Atrazine is made primarily by Syngenta.

item.title: Manager at Trump’s N.J. Golf Club Helped Plan Reflecting Pool Repairs
item.description: David Schutzenhofer, a Trump Organization employee, reached out to one of the vendors the government later hired for the iconic site near the Lincoln Memorial, documents show.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool has been plagued for decades by leaks and algae blooms, which various administrations have been unable to permanently fix.

item.title: Rising Energy Costs and Data Centers at Heart of NextEra’s Dominion Bid
item.description: NextEra’s proposed acquisition of Dominion Energy comes as Americans are paying a lot more for electricity, and data centers are demanding a lot more power from utilities.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Tristan Spinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: NextEra Energy’s acquisition of Dominion Energy would consolidate utility operations along much of the East Coast, which could shape electricity service for tens of millions of customers.

item.title: Tesla’s Semi Truck could Jolt the Trucking Industry
item.description: California truckers have expressed strong interest in the Tesla Semi because it costs much less and can travel further on a charge than electric trucks sold by established manufacturers.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Gabriela Bhaskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Screens on either side of a Tesla Semi’s steering wheel provide a view of the traffic around the vehicle.

item.title: Bipartisan Bill Would Impose New Annual Fee on Electric Vehicles
item.description: A House transportation bill introduced this week would require owners of electric cars to pay $130 to cover the cost of road repairs.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Congress is considering imposing a new annual fee on electric vehicles to help pay for road repairs.

item.title: She Was Finding Sources of Dangerous Water and Soil Pollution
item.description: Melanie Malone led a research project to identify and study contamination sites in Washington State. Then the E.P.A. canceled her grant.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Meron Tekie Menghistab for The New York Times

item.title: Scientists Tweaked the Global Warming Outlook. So Trump Weighed In.
item.description: Renewable energy has helped make the worst-case scenario a bit less bad. The president said, falsely, it shows that climate scientists were wrong all along.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:description: Solar panels and wind turbines in north-central China. Renewable is becoming more affordable in many countries.

item.title: E.P.A. to Repeal Some Limits on ‘Forever Chemicals’ in Drinking Water
item.description: The rules were established by the Biden administration after research linked the compounds to a range of serious health problems.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, has argued that the agency made a procedural error when it established the drinking water standards in 2024.

item.title: Ten people. Eight weeks. Three thousand feet through the ice.
item.description: Here’s the story of the expedition’s most ambitious project.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times

item.title: When Tornado Weather Hits, These Scientists Break Out the Colored Pencils
item.description: With a battery of modern technology at their fingertips, meteorologists often turn first to an old-fashioned tracking technique.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones and Desiree Rios

item.title: The Quest for Clean Hydrogen Moves Underground
item.description: The dream of clean hydrogen has tantalized energy experts for years, but producing it has been tough. Many start-ups think the answer could lie beneath our feet.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Ian Willms
